summary: Gruber revisits his initial reaction to Jony Ive's departure from Apple, offering a more nuanced reading of the organizational changes. He argues that having design chiefs report to COO Jeff Williams makes more sense than it first appeared, because Williams is effectively being repositioned as chief product officer rather than merely an operations executive. The key missed detail was Apple's simultaneous promotion of Sabih Khan to SVP of operations, which frees Williams from day-to-day operations work. Gruber emphasizes that titles at Apple don't accurately describe roles — what matters is rank and actual responsibility. He concludes that this restructuring may be a more thoughtful succession plan than simply replacing Ive with another chief design officer.
key_points: Apple's promotion of Sabih Khan to SVP of operations signals that Jeff Williams is no longer running operations, despite retaining the COO title
Williams is effectively becoming Apple's chief product officer, having led the Apple Watch from inception and now overseeing broader product development
At Apple, executive titles don't describe actual roles — COO has historically meant 'second in command' rather than literally running operations
Design reporting to Williams makes organizational sense when understood as design reporting to the product chief, not the operations chief
money_quotes: Williams still holds the title COO, but titles don't mean much at Apple.
Tim Cook didn't move design under operations; he promoted Williams to a new position, effectively 'chief product officer', and as such it makes sense that Hankey and Dye would report to him.
What 'COO' really means at Apple is 'second in command'.
I don't know where one would begin crafting a succinct title that accurately describes Cue's domain, but that's not it. That just doesn't matter at Apple.
Only time will tell if that's a better structure than replacing Jony Ive with a new chief design officer. But I feel a lot better about it than I did last week when the news broke.
key_insight: Apple's post-Ive design restructuring is better understood not as subordinating design to operations, but as elevating Jeff Williams to a de facto chief product officer role, with a new operations SVP taking over his former responsibilities.
